agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers at shift start, with dynamic recalculation triggers, customer notification windows, vehicle class enforcement, and configurable stop duration. This is a core operational feature spanning multiple bounded contexts: route planning, fleet/driver management, delivery lifecycle, and customer communication.",
    "bounded_context": "This feature spans at least four bounded contexts: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization, recalculation triggers, and stop sequencing; (2) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, vehicle class licensing, zone assignments, and shift lifecycle; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, assigned, in-progress, completed, failed), zone classification, and package characteristics including required vehicle class; (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers, delivery proximity windows, and customer communication preferences. The spec conflates these contexts, which is a design risk.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shift", "definition": "A bounded work period for a driver, beginning when the driver signals availability and ending at sign-off. A shift is the lifecycle anchor for route assignment." },
      { "term": "Zone", "definition": "A geographic operational area to which both drivers and deliveries are assigned. Zones are the partitioning unit for route optimization scope." },
      { "term": "Optimized Route", "definition": "An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated by the system to minimize travel time/distance within a zone for a specific driver's active shift." },
      { "term": "Pending Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that has been created and assigned to a zone but not yet assigned to a driver's route." },
      { "term": "Failed Delivery", "definition": "A delivery attempt that did not result in successful handoff to the recipient, triggering route recalculation for remaining stops." },
      { "term": "Licensed Vehicle Class", "definition": "The category of vehicle a driver is authorized to operate (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight). Determines which deliveries may be assigned to the driver based on the shipment's required vehicle class." },
      { "term": "Required Vehicle Class", "definition": "The minimum or specific vehicle class a delivery/shipment requires based on size, weight, or handling needs. This is a property of the delivery, not the driver." },
      { "term": "Next 3 Stops Window", "definition": "A proximity trigger: when a delivery is sequenced within the next 3 stops on the active route, the customer receives a notification." },
      { "term": "Route Deviation", "definition": "When a driver's actual GPS position or stop sequence diverges from the system-suggested route beyond a defined threshold, triggering recalculation." },
      { "term": "Stop Duration", "definition": "The estimated time a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes globally but is overridable per zone by the operations team." },
      { "term": "Recalculation Trigger", "definition": "An event that invalidates the current optimized route and requires recomputation: new delivery added, delivery marked failed, or driver deviation." },
      { "term": "Operations Team", "definition": "Internal users with authority to configure operational parameters such as per-zone stop duration overrides." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A driver MUST only be assigned deliveries whose required vehicle class is compatible with the driver's licensed vehicle class.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Violating this creates physical impossibility (a motorcycle cannot carry freight), safety hazards, and potential regulatory/insurance violations. This is a hard constraint rooted in physical reality and law.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery D assigned to driver X on route R: D.requiredVehicleClass ∈ X.licensedVehicleClasses"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "A driver MUST only be assigned deliveries within their assigned zone during that shift.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Zone assignment is a partitioning constraint that ensures geographic coherence of routes. Cross-zone assignment would corrupt route optimization and violate operational boundaries.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery D on driver X's route: D.zone == X.currentShift.zone"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "When a driver starts a shift, the system automatically generates an optimized route from all pending deliveries in the driver's zone that are compatible with the driver's vehicle class.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is operational policy — the business could decide to allow manual route creation or batch assignment instead. The automation is a product decision, not a physical or data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Within T seconds of shift start, an optimized route exists for the driver containing only pending, zone-matched, vehicle-class-compatible deliveries."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a new pending delivery is added to the driver's active zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an optimization policy. The business could choose to batch recalculations or defer them. It does not corrupt data if skipped — it just produces suboptimal routes.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an active route for driver X in zone Z, when a new delivery is created in zone Z, a recalculation event is triggered and the route is updated to include the new delivery."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a delivery on the active route is marked as failed.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Operational policy for route freshness. A stale route with a failed stop is suboptimal but not corrupt.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an active route containing delivery D, when D is marked failed, the route is recalculated excluding D from remaining stops."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Operational policy. The driver can still complete deliveries on a stale route — recalculation is an optimization, not an integrity requirement.",
      "testable_assertion": "When the system detects the driver's actual position or stop sequence has deviated beyond the defined threshold, a recalculation is triggered using the driver's current position as the new origin."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Customers are notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window on the driver's active route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Notification policy. Missing a notification degrades customer experience but does not corrupt delivery state or route integrity.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a delivery D transitions from position > 3 to position ≤ 3 on the active route (or is initially placed at position ≤ 3), exactly one notification is sent to D's customer."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The default estimated stop duration is 4 minutes per delivery stop.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a configurable operational parameter, explicitly described as overridable. A VP could change it tomorrow.",
      "testable_assertion": "When no zone-level override exists, the route optimizer uses 240 seconds as the stop duration for ETA calculations."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "The operations team can override the estimated stop duration on a per-zone basis.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Administrative configuration policy. The override mechanism itself is a product feature, not an integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a zone Z has a stop duration override of N minutes, all route calculations in zone Z use N minutes instead of the 4-minute default."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver starts a shift in zone Z with licensed vehicle class 'van', and there are 10 pending deliveries in zone Z (8 requiring 'van' or smaller, 2 requiring 'truck')",
      "when": "The shift begins",
      "then": "An optimized route is generated containing exactly the 8 compatible deliveries; the 2 truck deliveries are excluded"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with 7 remaining stops",
      "when": "A new pending delivery is added to the driver's zone and is compatible with their vehicle class",
      "then": "The route is recalculated to include the new delivery, re-optimizing the sequence of all remaining (undelivered) stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and delivery at stop 4 is marked as failed",
      "when": "The failure is recorded",
      "then": "The route is recalculated, the failed delivery is removed from the active route, and remaining stops are re-optimized from the driver's current position"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver deviates from their suggested route (e.g., skips a stop or takes a different physical path beyond threshold)",
      "when": "The deviation is detected",
      "then": "The route is recalculated from the driver's current position with all remaining undelivered stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver's active route has deliveries A, B, C, D, E in sequence and the driver just completed a delivery",
      "when": "Delivery D moves from position 4 to position 3 (entering the 'next 3 stops' window)",
      "then": "Customer for delivery D receives a proximity notification"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A route is recalculated and delivery F was at position 5 but is now at position 2",
      "when": "The recalculated route is activated",
      "then": "Customer for delivery F receives a proximity notification (entered the window due to recalculation, not just stop completion)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "Zone Z has a stop duration override of 6 minutes and zone Y has no override",
      "when": "Routes are calculated for drivers in both zones",
      "then": "Zone Z routes use 6 minutes per stop for ETAs; zone Y routes use the 4-minute default"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A motorcycle driver is active in zone Z",
      "when": "A freight shipment (requiring 'truck' class) is added to zone Z",
      "then": "The freight shipment is NOT added to the motorcycle driver's route, even during recalculation"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-9",
      "given": "A customer has already been notified that their delivery is in the 'next 3 stops' window",
      "when": "A recalculation occurs but the delivery remains within the next 3 stops",
      "then": "The customer is NOT notified again (no duplicate notification)"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Multiple drivers active in the same zone at shift start", "expected_behavior": "Pending deliveries must be partitioned across drivers — not duplicated. The spec is silent on how deliveries are distributed among multiple concurrent drivers in the same zone. This needs clarification: is it first-come-first-served, load-balanced, or proximity-based?" },
    { "scenario": "A recalculation is triggered while a previous recalculation is still in progress", "expected_behavior": "The system must either queue recalculation triggers or cancel-and-restart. Concurrent recalculations for the same route must not produce conflicting states." },
    { "scenario": "Driver's only remaining stop is marked as failed", "expected_behavior": "Route becomes empty. The system should transition the driver to an 'awaiting deliveries' or 'idle' state rather than leaving them on an empty route." },
    { "scenario": "New delivery added to zone but all active drivers in that zone have incompatible vehicle classes", "expected_behavior": "The delivery remains in 'pending' state, unassigned. The system should surface this to the operations team as an unassignable delivery alert." },
    { "scenario": "Driver deviates, triggering recalculation, but simultaneously a delivery in their route is marked failed", "expected_behavior": "Both triggers should result in a single recalculation that accounts for both changes — the deviation origin AND the removed failed delivery." },
    { "scenario": "Customer notification sent for 'next 3 stops', then recalculation moves delivery back to position 5", "expected_behavior": "The customer was already notified of proximity. Spec is silent on whether a 'delay' or 'update' notification should be sent. This is a CX-critical gap." },
    { "scenario": "Driver starts shift but there are zero pending deliveries in their zone", "expected_behavior": "Driver enters shift with an empty route and transitions to 'awaiting deliveries' state. Route is generated when first delivery arrives in their zone." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is reassigned from one zone to another mid-route", "expected_behavior": "If the delivery was on an active route, it must be removed and both routes (source and destination zone) should recalculate." },
    { "scenario": "Operations team changes zone stop duration while drivers have active routes", "expected_behavior": "Spec is silent on whether active routes are recalculated with new stop duration or only future routes are affected. This impacts live ETA accuracy." },
    { "scenario": "What constitutes a 'deviation' — GPS drift vs. intentional detour", "expected_behavior": "A deviation threshold must be defined (distance and/or sequence mismatch). GPS noise must not trigger constant recalculations. The spec does not define the threshold." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, increases deliveries-per-shift (driver utilization), and reduces overtime. Incorrect vehicle class assignment could result in failed deliveries, wasted trips, and potential insurance/liability claims. The 4-minute default stop duration, if poorly calibrated, directly impacts ETA accuracy and capacity planning.",
    "operational": "This feature is a force multiplier for dispatch operations — replacing manual route planning with automated optimization. However, the recalculation triggers (3 distinct events) introduce computational load that must be managed. The multi-driver-per-zone partitioning gap is the highest operational risk: without clear assignment logic, two drivers could receive overlapping routes or deliveries could be left unassigned.",
    "user": "Customers gain transparency via the 'next 3 stops' notification, reducing 'where is my package' inquiries. Drivers gain efficiency via optimized routes but lose some autonomy (deviations trigger recalculation). The recalculation-after-deviation behavior must feel helpful, not punitive — UX framing matters.",
    "risk": "Highest risk is the multi-driver zone assignment gap — this is not a minor edge case but a core operational scenario. Secondary risk is recalculation storm potential: a busy zone with frequent new deliveries could trigger continuous recalculations, degrading system performance and driver experience. Third risk is notification accuracy — sending 'almost there' and then bumping the delivery back creates customer trust erosion.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "Primary workflow change — shifts from manual route selection to system-assigned routes. Deviation detection may feel like surveillance if not framed carefully. Positive: less planning burden, more deliveries completed." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Gain real-time proximity notifications. Risk of notification inaccuracy if recalculations frequently shuffle the route. Expectation management is critical." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "Gains zone-level configuration authority (stop duration). Needs visibility into unassignable deliveries, route quality metrics, and recalculation frequency. This team is the safety net for edge cases." },
      { "group": "Dispatch/Planning", "impact": "Significant reduction in manual workload. Must trust the optimizer or will shadow-plan manually, undermining the feature." },
      { "group": "Finance/Fleet", "impact": "Fuel cost reduction, better asset utilization metrics. Vehicle class enforcement reduces misallocation and insurance risk." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec has two significant gaps that prevent high confidence: (1) Multi-driver zone assignment — the spec describes optimization for 'a driver' but real zones will have multiple concurrent drivers. How deliveries are partitioned is a fundamental design question left unanswered. (2) Deviation threshold definition — 'manually deviates' is undefined; without a threshold, this rule is untestable. Additionally, the notification-then-reorder scenario has no specified behavior, creating a customer experience ambiguity.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Vehicle class enforcement invariant (BR-1) — unambiguous physical constraint",
      "Zone containment rule (BR-2) — clear geographic partitioning",
      "Stop duration default and override mechanism (BR-8, BR-9) — well-specified",
      "Individual recalculation triggers (BR-4, BR-5, BR-6) — clear event-based rules"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Multi-driver delivery assignment/partitioning within a zone",
      "Deviation detection threshold and definition",
      "Customer notification behavior when recalculation moves delivery OUT of the 3-stop window",
      "Recalculation throttling/debouncing policy under high trigger frequency",
      "Whether zone stop duration changes apply to active routes immediately"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A driver has exactly one active shift at a time",
      "A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift",
      "A delivery belongs to exactly one zone",
      "Vehicle class compatibility is a strict hierarchy or set membership, not a fuzzy match",
      "The 'next 3 stops' window is based on route sequence position, not geographic proximity or time",
      "Failed deliveries are removed from the route entirely (not re-queued for retry on the same route)",
      "Route optimization considers travel distance/time but the specific algorithm is not a domain concern"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec describes route generation for 'a driver' but does not address how pending deliveries are partitioned when MULTIPLE drivers are active in the same zone simultaneously. This is not an edge case — it is the normal operating condition for any busy zone. Without a partitioning strategy (load-balanced, proximity-based, capacity-based), the feature cannot be correctly implemented for production use.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Load-balanced — distribute deliveries evenly across active drivers by count",
        "B: Proximity-based — assign each delivery to the nearest driver, then optimize per-driver",
        "C: Capacity-based — fill one driver's route before assigning to the next",
        "D: Combined — use proximity + vehicle class + current load as a scoring function"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option D (combined scoring) is the industry standard for multi-driver dispatch optimization, but this requires explicit business input on weighting factors."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "What constitutes a 'manual deviation' from the route? The spec says recalculation triggers on deviation but does not define the threshold. Is it GPS distance from the planned path? Visiting stops out of sequence? Skipping a stop? Without a clear definition, this rule is untestable and risks either never triggering (threshold too high) or triggering constantly from GPS noise (threshold too low).",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Sequence-based — deviation means visiting stops out of the suggested order",
        "B: Distance-based — deviation means GPS position is > N meters from planned path for > M seconds",
        "C: Combined — either sequence deviation OR significant geographic deviation triggers recalculation"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C (combined) with configurable thresholds per zone, similar to the stop duration override. Sequence deviation is simpler to detect and more actionable; distance deviation catches detours."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "When a recalculation moves a delivery OUT of the 'next 3 stops' window after the customer has already been notified of proximity, what happens? The customer received 'your delivery is almost here' and now it's been pushed back. This is a customer trust issue that the spec does not address.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Send no update — customer received the initial notification and will eventually get the delivery",
        "B: Send a delay/update notification — 'your delivery has been rescheduled, new ETA: X'",
        "C: Lock deliveries in the 3-stop window — once notified, the delivery cannot be moved back in the queue"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B for transparency, but Option C should be seriously considered as it prevents the problem entirely at the cost of some optimization flexibility."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
